item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never heard nor read the tale of Frosty the Snowman (Blame it on living in a South American country in a city where it has only snowed twice in 200 years), so I don't get any "Easter egg" connection that there may be. The skip is boring and derivative, everything about it has been done before with greater success. I really don't know how I would improve it. Maybe it's just not my cup of tea.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Link to image source is here.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

There are currently three MTFs mentioned in this article that all have entries on the MTF page. These are, Iota-10, Mu-4, and Pi-1 Simple and non-intrusive means of facilitating the wikiwalk here would be to turn these mentions into crosslinks. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Anomalous animation events fitting SCP-2890's profile have been added to the MTF Mu-4 ("Debuggers") internet watch list to be flagged as a possible containment breach.

To this:

Anomalous animation events fitting SCP-2890's profile have been added to the MTF Mu-4 ("Debuggers") internet watch list to be flagged as a possible containment breach.

Changing this:

In the event of a confirmed breach by SCP-2890, MTF Pi-1 ("City Slickers") is to mobilize and recover the artifact. If it is not located at the GPS coordinates, the search should continue with the public parks, elementary schools, preschools, and day-care centers within 50 km of Site-28 (see Supplement 2890-C) and expand from there, focusing on locations where prepubescent children congregate.

To this:

In the event of a confirmed breach by SCP-2890, MTF Pi-1 ("City Slickers") is to mobilize and recover the artifact. If it is not located at the GPS coordinates, the search should continue with the public parks, elementary schools, preschools, and day-care centers within 50 km of Site-28 (see Supplement 2890-C) and expand from there, focusing on locations where prepubescent children congregate.

Changing this:

SCP-2890 was initially located when a group of seven children from Saratoga Springs, New York went missing. When found three weeks later they all claimed that they had been learning to dance from a plush duck toy. The anthropomorphic toy, wearing SCP-2890, was recovered by MTF Iota-10 ("Damn Feds") agents operating as part of the FBI investigation into the possible mass kidnapping. The toy was determined to be non-anomalous and destroyed.

To this:

SCP-2890 was initially located when a group of seven children from Saratoga Springs, New York went missing. When found three weeks later they all claimed that they had been learning to dance from a plush duck toy. The anthropomorphic toy, wearing SCP-2890, was recovered by MTF Iota-10 ("Damn Feds") agents operating as part of the FBI investigation into the possible mass kidnapping. The toy was determined to be non-anomalous and destroyed.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could be!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, I keep thinking that the magician in the hat also made SCP-573.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh hey, thanks (I was camping when you posted and missed this)! I feel like this was a really good concept that got hampered by an exceptionally ham-handed execution (I think I took a lot of very good advice exactly wrong). I am glad it is finally finding an audience!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not actually literally Satanic in any sense except the most general Christian moral absolutist one, no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Satanic frosty the Snowman in summery

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for this little treasure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

Interesting idea about the mother's command.

Many thanks too for indulging my request!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was intending some ambiguity there. Is it saying this because Asling is a woman and thinks that the Foundation is a government agency? Is it misogynistic? Does it mean that it hates adults?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm guessing it's that a mother's command will override the compulsion.

I began to wonder when you mentioned the charm in the hat. I was 90% sure when it had symbols on it. Tying it to dancing (much as the fae circles) just cinched it. Well done, slow build, believable for the most part. And it never outright mentioned a snowman. I kept waiting for it.

+1 for successfully making a childhood image terrifying and having me enjoy it.

Edit: sorry for any offense. My response was jokingly based on the "George Lucas…" version. Except I didn't enjoy most of what was done to that part of my childhood. Yours was fun!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given what it wants to "teach" the children, I can see why it would hate governments, but why would also hate doctors and women?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I paired it back to what it was (mostly).

I'm starting to feel like I would have been better off just cold-posting this. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Cranking them into the D-Class system for the duration of this… is bad juju.

The alternative, that adult refugees are but their children are not is even more deeply weird, though and the description of D-Class says it includes refugees.

I got the impression that this experiment took place outside of that procedure, as a temporary measure,

I can't see how that works given the way the Foundation operates. Human subjects are "D-Class". Anybody else is either too valuable to experiment on, or a civilian.

and that they were released to their ordinary lives and observed.

I think that they get settled by the Foundation somewhere actually, rather than being returned to displaced person status. That would make the most sense, and I tried to imply that.

but not even remotely ok with them slapping refugee kids into a system that, headcanon or not, implies mandatory termination.

Well, mandatory termination of innocent refuges was already bad, children or not. I'm just going to ignore that honestly and assume that this kind of D-Class (which really has to be the majority anyway) get an automatic waiver.

If I had my druthers, I'd think there out to be a couple different grades of experimental subjects, including animals, the terminally ill, non-disposable, and then "regular" D-Class. It would solve a lot of these problems.

I can't see the Foundation balking at experimenting on children if it feels it needs to (especially with something that appears to be non-injurious like this). Note that I have them end the experiments after test 21.

You could delete both of these addenda and I think it would be an improvement.

In which case this gets paired down to what it was like before I got any feedback. That doesn't seem right. Apparently people felt that there wasn't enough exposition, and thought that the Foundation not trying to research this stuff made them look incompetent. I'm now pretty confused as to what I can do, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That puts it very close to what it was like in the very first draft before I got any feedback… You don't think that it leaves too much unexplained?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, so!

Collapsible!
– hide block

Overall, it's a little cheesy, but you eventually managed to sell me on it. And then, unfortunately, unsold me.

So, addenda 1 and 2, at the time of this writing:

Analysis of Imaging Studies
Test 21

I liked. I think they were solid worldbuilding and sold me on the creep factor, which was important and difficult, because this is Frosty's hat, and that's hard for me to take seriously.

Addenda 3

Interview of D-7920

Was… ok, but a big personal turn-off. I don't like that you referred to the child from Group 2 as a D-Class. I got the impression that this experiment took place outside of that procedure, as a temporary measure, and that they were released to their ordinary lives and observed. Cranking them into the D-Class system for the duration of this… is bad juju.

I'm VERY ok with the Foundation accidentally messing up these kids psychologically and only finding out later, but not even remotely ok with them slapping refugee kids into a system that, headcanon or not, implies mandatory termination. Especially for something that isn't a Keter.

Addenda 4 and 5:

Interview of SCP-2890
Memorandum

Were completely superfluous. You sold me on the horror in addenda 2, which, as I said, was a significant accomplishment. You don't need to spike it in with an interview log shouting "I'M EVIL!" and an explicit description of what should be fridge horror. You could delete both of these addenda and I think it would be an improvement.

-1 for now, for the personal squick of the interview and the unnecessary/excessive narrative repetition of the last two addenda. I would easily change my vote if pared back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure that I like the additions. The article was doing fine without them, and the connection to that canon is really tenuous and just feels tacked on.

I'm leaving my upvote in place, but I really do think you should just leave out the new additions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I deleted Lavender Circle and moved most of the salient bits into the main article. Hopefully that will help. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I added a sentence that should help, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy Frosty. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not saying that the luck thing should go; I'm saying that it could be expressed better and tie in the article.

While one breach based on coincidences might be written off as an unfortunate event, multiple breaches surrounding that particular item is less likely to be random events and one may then conjecture that the item is somehow causing those events to occur. It's a decent interpretation of chaos magic, I'd admit.

So yeah… I'm advocating for multiple breaches to justify the luck thing and amended procedures. By stating that those breaches are grounded in mistakes and odd coincidences (without saying what really went down) and multiple events to further the item's "goals", it might better convey the message that the item is manipulating probability for its own ends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well without the luck thing, or something, I can't see how this thing isn't destroyed, lost or contained long ago. Besides "luck" of this kind is a typical sort of magic that practitioners of this kind believe in.

I did write an alternative draft where it lacked this and was Safe contained in the 30s but almost nobody liked it.

As to the coincidence thing, I'm not sure if one incident can establish that the hat manipulates probability. If it commits multiple breaches based on "accidents" and "coincidences", then these breaches might be as a result of the hat and not pure extremely dumb luck.

Well, the statistical analysis and the existence of the "luck" charm was supposed to make it a fairly sensible conclusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My apologies, forgot to place the coding. But I would argue that the breach is not where the meat of the article is (hence, I do not find the amended procedures justified and treated properly in the document); it should be the Hanged King correlation. I would like to see how it is incorporated into this hat of yours (yeah, the supplement doesn't really do that all that well, I'm afraid…)

As to the coincidence thing, I'm not sure if one incident can establish that the hat manipulates probability. If it commits multiple breaches based on "accidents" and "coincidences", then these breaches might be as a result of the hat and not pure extremely dumb luck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
distinct combination of coincidences that are too coincidental to be by chance

Yes, that is exactly what I say it is.

What is it you think it was supposed to be?

So they had deduced that based on one breach that appeared more like a series of accidents and coincidences.

Plus the luck charm yes. Also that's 1036 not 1036. Really there ought to be even more steps but really that would only make it more boring to read, IMO. The point is that these are so unlikely by chance that it is statically much more likely to be correlated with the "luck" charm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I see the circumstances around this "breach", I can see that it is very ridiculous and it relies on a distinct combination of coincidences that are too coincidental to be by chance. It feels more like an accident due to incompetence, rather than an anomalous aspect of the item that breached containment.

It is estimated that the odds of this sequence of events happening by random chance alone are less than 1-in-1036. As a result, the object is now believed to possess the ability to manipulate probability, causing unlikely coincidences and accidents that are serendipitous to the artifact's presumed goals.

So they had deduced that based on one breach that appeared more like a series of accidents and coincidences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But those "real world practices" were not real magic.

I don't understand this at all. It's in the real world. It's magic. The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn was/is real.

Mixing them in a blender without thought will have no meaningful result.

I think the premise was that the will of the magician was the important part, the symbols are only there for him to focus on. So whatever he thinks should work, will work. Besides, not mixing them also has no meaningful result in the real world.

I mean, if the procedures are going to be there, I would honestly expect what happened that lead to such measures.

There was a series of unlikely events that all individually contributed. I wrote (I thought) a fun version that everybody hated. I tried to write a less silly version, but it was really boring.

+ Boring?
- Yep, boring.

time: Secondary fault check circuits deactivated because of rewiring.
time: A bucket of used supplies from SCP- 602 was placed in the vault pending analysis.
time: Researcher [Redacted] returned SPC-2890 to containment and did not secure the lock properly but lock showed as locked to the system.
time: Vibrations in the building harmonized with the resonance frequency of the bottom of the locker for 31 seconds. Artifact vibrated out of the locker. The bucket of supplies tipped over.
time: Positive airflow was restored to the vault. Pressure closed the locker door (which successfully latched) and pushed the artifact into the bucket of used supplies.
time: Researcher [Redacted] removed the bucket. On duty vault technician [Redacted] failed to inspect the bucket properly and permitted it to leave the vault.
time: Power to the entire facility failed due to Electrician [Redacted]'s error. Researcher [Redacted] slipped and fell down the stairwell in the dark, dropping the bucket. The bucket rolled down to the ground floor, and in the darkness the hat was dislodged and rolled into the entrance gate area. Emergency power was restored.
time: Hat fell into Security Officer [Redacted['s bag which had already been searched as she was going off-duty and leaving the facility.
time: Entrance gate was secured until main power restored. Security Officer [Redacted] zipped up her bag while waiting for main doors to unlock.
time: Main power restored. Security Officer [Redacted] left the facility.
time: Security Officer [Redacted]'s bag was stolen while on the subway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did… you… just pull a skip out of "Frosty the Snowman"?

I…

Take your +1, you dirty bird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was a reference to real world practices that do this very same thing.

But those "real world practices" were not real magic. Besides, looking at Western and Eastern occult systems, they run on vastly different parameters (i.e. the 5 elements in Classical and Chinese alchemy). Mixing them in a blender without thought will have no meaningful result.

As for your second point, I still don't know the context as to why the item requires such strict security measures and I don't see that aspect covered in this article o the supplement. This (and the initial breach) does not really add up to me as a coherent article.

I mean, if the procedures are going to be there, I would honestly expect what happened that lead to such measures. And if I'm not wrong, I didn't see the justifications behind current containment procedures or how it can breach containment given its anomalous properties.

Final call: Downvote… and hope for improvement

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ You mad, jammy bastard!
- MELT, DAMN YOU!

You made Frosty's hat an item. I adore you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the interviews and am glad you remove the over-the-top occult references (I don't think that mixing too much "thaumaturgical systems" are conducive to functional magic).

It was a reference to real world practices that do this very same thing.
'

However, I'm not sure about what you mean of the SCP's ability to escape. It is not actually shown how it escaped in the first place, which could be quite a story on its own.

Well there once was a log, but generally everybody unanimously hated it, so I replaced it with the summary in the description. I tried to write a less OTT sequence of events, but it was just long and boring to read (and amounted to " during a comprehensive upgrade of Site-28's electrical systems, a combination of simultaneous unrelated errors by personnel and undetected failures of the security systems").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm giving this article the most tentative of upvotes, but I'm going to have to downvote Lavender Circle in its current form.

That's bad, because I wrote it to answer all of your questions. I'm not sure what is wrong with it now. :(

Irony.

Intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last addendum pushed me to an upvote. Some rough edges, but you pulled it off for me.
+1 for debasing a beloved part of my childhood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm giving this article the most tentative of upvotes, but I'm going to have to downvote Lavender Circle in its current form.

+ I noticed this...
- ... in LAVENDER CIRCLE

we just got lucky

Irony.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the interviews and am glad you remove the over-the-top occult references (I don't think that mixing too much "thaumaturgical systems" are conducive to functional magic).

However, I'm not sure about what you mean of the SCP's ability to escape. It is not actually shown how it escaped in the first place, which could be quite a story on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept enough to not downvote, but it wasn't as rewarding to read for me to upvote. No-vote, for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, but the point is that you don't say it outright. The fact is there, but you don't confirm or deny it in the article itself, leaving the readers free to think what they want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is all better to the point where I can no-vote. Not sure about the interview; I'll look at it with a more awake mind tomorrow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still, you didn't put it on a snowman, cliche averted, all's well.

I hate to dissuade you from my one upvote, but I feel like I ought to tell you:

+ show block
– hide block

It is supposed to be the hat that inspired that song.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, that's some deep discussion in the comments. And here I am, and this is my opinion:

A hat that makes things come alive? That is fun! Why didn't we have that yet? +1

…I must be childish. Still, you didn't put it on a snowman, cliche averted, all's well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I regret not commenting on this in draft form, as the title caught my eye, but I never had the time to dedicate myself to this.

Me too. :(

This seems like a way to say "It's spooky and creepy and really mysterious. Look at all these symbols that have no ties to them except how mystic they are!" and it bothered me enough that I had to write this comment the moment I read it.

Well it was supposed to be like the stuff that all those Hermetic Magick guys were doing around the turn of the 20th century, except this actually works. The point was so that you'd say, "Ah this guy was some kind of Crowley-like guy".

It is also supposed to be a bit of versimilitude. "Some generic mystical symbols" would not have been very good either.

The interview with D-7920 seems… I'm not sure how to peg it. It doesn't seem believable. I read it back to myself and I can't actually imagine anyone speaking like that person does.

That is ungood.
She's supposed to be a little girl, who's somewhat brainwashed, and translated from Arabic. I'll reread it and see if I can figure out where it went wrong.

The attempt to connect 701 with this solely because of the Alagadda connection feels immensely shoehorned, and provides absolutely nothing.

I can't imagine Lavender Circle not looking into it, honestly. They wouldn't be doing their jobs if they just ignored the lead.

as does the 1274 mention; while I appreciate bringing attention to underrated skips, that thing is entirely different from this. It seems like you want to connect them because these are both dance skips?

It's a dance that makes you violent and hedonistic, isn't it? Perhaps I overreached, I'll take a look again.

I'm not sure what this is supposed to be saying. Is this suddenly political commentary, or does it have a political bent? What's the relation to this line with everything else? That a tophat is the reason why the world is suddenly more "open-minded"? You had the horror working fine before this, and with this you complicate things to the point where I'm not sure of your intentions.

This (and really all of Lavender Circle) came from me trying to answer questions that people kept asking. In this case it ended up there because people kept saying that this wasn't really very threatening. I think I may have overstated it.

EDIT: Made some changes to Lavender Circle.
EDIT2: Simplified the symbology report. Better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I regret not commenting on this in draft form, as the title caught my eye, but I never had the time to dedicate myself to this. Here are some things that stick out to me:

The symbols are from a large variety of sources, including Kabbalah, Hermetic mysticism, Enochian lexiurgy, European, Chinese, and Arabic alchemy, Icelandic nábrókarstafur, elder rune workings, and Daevasite fate-bindings.

This seems like a way to say "It's spooky and creepy and really mysterious. Look at all these symbols that have no ties to them except how mystic they are!" and it bothered me enough that I had to write this comment the moment I read it.

The interview with D-7920 seems… I'm not sure how to peg it. It doesn't seem believable. I read it back to myself and I can't actually imagine anyone speaking like that person does.

From LAVENDER CIRCLE:

Following the connection to Alagadda, LAVENDER CIRCLE attempted to correlate SCP-2890 with SCP-701. No positive correlation could be made, however ██████ ██████, the editor of the 1813 Cambridge University Press edition, is an anagram for █████ ███████; this was found to be only of marginal statistical significance.

The attempt to connect 701 with this solely because of the Alagadda connection feels immensely shoehorned, and provides absolutely nothing. You could cut this and the article would be better off for it (as does the 1274 mention; while I appreciate bringing attention to underrated skips, that thing is entirely different from this. It seems like you want to connect them because these are both dance skips?).

We certainly live in a more sensuous, hedonistic, and open-minded society now than we had when this thing was made.

I'm not sure what this is supposed to be saying. Is this suddenly political commentary, or does it have a political bent? What's the relation to this line with everything else? That a tophat is the reason why the world is suddenly more "open-minded"? You had the horror working fine before this, and with this you complicate things to the point where I'm not sure of your intentions.

Taking both documents into account, I have to downvote this. You go for something, you seem like you're almost there (even if some parts are a bit weird), and you end up fumbling before you can realize it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image is "Collapsible Top Hat" from Wikimedia Commons.

Thanks to Mister Ious, TwistedGears, spikebrennan, j0si, and ironiclegacy for critiques and proofreading (and I take full responsibility for what I chose to ignore)!

Special thanks to Metaphysician for help with the world building!
